scenarios.title: Fix the React counter
scenarios.brief: The increment function is commented out and uses invalid state updates. Uncomment it and fix it so the counter works correctly.
instructions: Use the setter (setCount) instead of mutating count directly.
The fix should follow React's rules for updating state.
scenarios.hint: In React, you must use the state setter function (e.g. setCount) to update state. Direct assignment like count = count + 1 does not trigger a re-render.
scenarios.whyItMatters: Using the state setter ensures React tracks changes and re-renders the component. Direct mutation is not allowed.

scenarios.title: Fix the Docker build command
scenarios.brief: Your team is trying to build a Docker image, but the command fails because it is missing required syntax. Add the image tag and build context.
instructions: Add an image tag so the image has a readable name (use -t).
Specify the build context so Docker knows which files to include (e.g. . for current directory).
scenarios.hint: Use -t to tag the image (e.g. -t my-node-app) and add a build context (e.g. .) at the end so Docker knows where to find the Dockerfile.
scenarios.whyItMatters: The -t flag names your image for later use; the build context (.) tells Docker where to find the Dockerfile and which files to include.

scenarios.title: Fix the async fetch
scenarios.brief: This code tries to fetch data but doesn't handle the Promise correctly. Fix it so the result is used asynchronously.
instructions: Use async/await or .then() to handle the Promise returned by fetch.
Use the response (e.g. parse JSON or log it) after the fetch completes.
scenarios.hint: fetch() returns a Promise. Use async/await: const res = await fetch(...); then use res. Remember to make the function async.
scenarios.whyItMatters: Async operations return Promises; you must await them or use .then() to use the result instead of the Promise object.

scenarios.title: Fix the Python type hint
scenarios.brief: The function is missing a return type hint and one argument type. Add the correct annotations so the type checker can validate the function.
instructions: Add a return type hint (the function returns an int).
Add a type hint for the parameter n.
scenarios.hint: Use -> int for the return type and n: int for the parameter. Example: def factorial(n: int) -> int:
scenarios.whyItMatters: Type hints document the contract of a function and enable static type checkers and editors to catch errors before runtime.
